t: Adding a web development tool to Safari Web Inspector
a: Expand the built-in Safari Web Inspector to include your custom tool, augmenting developers’ options for inspecting, testing, and debugging webpages.

t: Adjusting settings for a toolbar item
a: Customize a toolbar item for your Safari app extension.

t: Adjusting settings for contextual menu items
a: Customize contextual menu items for your Safari app extension.
Set website access permissions in a Safari app extension using information property list keys.

t: Adopting Declarative Content Blocking in Safari Web Extensions
a: Block web content with your web extension using the declarative net request API.

t: Adopting New Safari Web Extension APIs
a: Improve your web extension in Safari with a non-persistent background page and new tab-override customization.

t: Assessing your Safari web extension’s browser compatibility
a: Review your Safari web extension implementation plan, manifest keys, and JavaScript API usage for compatibility with other browsers.

t: Blocking content with your Safari web extension
a: Build content blocking with declarative net request into your Safari web extension.
Add, build, and enable a Safari app extension.

t: Building and testing a Safari web extension
a: Get started with Safari web extensions by creating one from the ground up, using any code editor.

t: Converting a Safari app extension to a Safari web extension
a: Unify your web extensions and simplify development by sharing code with a Safari web extension.

t: Converting a legacy Safari extension to a Safari app extension
a: Convert a legacy Safari extension to a Safari app extension, automatically with keys or manually.
Learn how to make custom Safari Web Inspector extensions.
Build a Safari web extension in Xcode.
Create a content blocker for Safari in Xcode.
Review unsupported symbols and their replacements.
Customize and enhance web pages by building a Safari web extension.
Get feedback on your extension from beta testers, then share your extension with customers in the App Store.
Transfer bookmarks, saved passwords, and other information between browsers.

t: Injecting CSS style sheets into a webpage
a: Add to or override styles by injecting CSS style sheets into webpages.

t: Injecting a script into a webpage
a: Inject a script that you write for a Safari app extension into a webpage.
Respect user privacy by setting appropriate permissions for your Safari web extension.

t: Messaging a Web Extension’s Native App
a: Communicate between your Safari web extension and its containing app.

t: Messaging between a webpage and your Safari web extension
a: Configure your extension to enable messaging from webpages, and then update your extension to handle messages.

t: Messaging between the app and JavaScript in a Safari web extension
a: Communicate about events and share data between the containing app and JavaScript by using native messaging and app groups.
Learn about enhancements to Safari Web Extensions.

t: Optimizing your web extension for Safari
a: Support Dark Mode, reduce memory and power usage, and ensure feature compatibility to improve your web extension experience in Safari and web apps.

t: Packaging a web extension for Safari
a: Package your existing extension as a Safari web extension using Xcode’s command-line tool.

t: Packaging and distributing Safari Web Extensions with App Store Connect
a: Upload and distribute Safari Web Extensions without using a Mac or Xcode.

t: Passing messages between Safari app extensions and injected scripts
a: Communicate between your Safari app extension and injected scripts.
Build a Safari Extension that displays metadata using Open Graph.

The domain for content blocker or Safari app extension errors.
A string the system uses as a key in a user info dictionary to identify a message.
A string the system uses as a key in a user info dictionary to identify a profile identifier.

An object that provides a visible standard interface for browsing the web.

t: Safari app extension information property list keys
a: Specify keys in your information property list file that provide information about your Safari app extension, UI, and permissions to the operating system.
Learn how Safari app extensions extend the web-browsing experience in Safari by leveraging web technologies and native code.
Create web extensions that work in Safari and other browsers.

t: Setting Safari app extension feature keys
a: Set keys for permissions, scripts, style sheets, contextual menu items, and toolbar items in the information property list file.

t: Syncing Safari web extensions across devices and platforms
a: Let users install your extension on one device and then use and manage the extension on all their other iOS and macOS devices.
Debug your Safari app extension with these techniques.
Use developer resources to investigate and resolve common problems with Safari web extensions.
Add new features and fix bugs in your Safari web extension using Xcode tools.

t: Using Safari app extension default keys
a: Learn about the default keys in the information property list file.

t: Using content script and style sheet keys
a: Learn about controlling style sheet and script injection with information property list keys.

t: Using contextual menu and toolbar item keys
a: Learn about adding contextual menu items and toolbar items to a Safari app extension with information property list keys.

t: Using injected style sheets and scripts
a: Learn how you can affect the appearance or behavior of a webpage by using injected style sheets and scripts.

t: Using permissions for scripts and style sheets
a: Learn about URL permissions for scripts and style sheets in a Safari app extension using information property list keys.

Tells the handler which resources a content blocker blocked on a webpage.
A method the system calls when a user selects one of the app extension’s context menu items.
Returns the Safari Reading List singleton object.
An object that provides behavior for the Safari view controller’s Done and Action buttons.
The style of dismiss button to use in the navigation bar to close the Safari view controller.
Sends a message to a Safari app extension, launching Safari if necessary.
Dispatches a message from the app extension to the content script injected in this page.
A value that specifies whether Safari should enter Reader mode, if it is available.
An object you use to send tap event attribution data to the browser for Private Click Measurement.
Calls the completion handler passing the active page in the tab.
Calls the completion handler with the active tab in the target window.
Calls the completion handler with the active browser window.
Provides information about a web app to the system.
Calls the completion handler with all of the tab’s active and preloading pages.
Retrieves the properties of the webpage.

Returns the current state of a Safari extension.

A Boolean value that indicates whether the page is currently active.
A Boolean value that indicates whether the content blocker is enabled.
A flag that indicates whether the universal link is enabled.
A Boolean value that indicates whether the extension is enabled.

A method the system calls when the extension receives a message from the extension’s containing app.
Presents the Export Browsing Data sheet from Safari Settings.
Presents the extensions pane from Safari Settings.
Opens a tab at the end of the tab bar.
Opens a new window with the desired webpage.

The color to tint the control buttons on the navigation bar and the toolbar.

The title of the page.
